title: marginplyr
text:
nest_with_margins() creates one nested data frame for every group in every grouping set. It works with local data frames and dtplyr steps.



nest_with_margins(
  .data,
  .by = NULL,
  .grouping = NULL,
  .margin_label = "Total",
  .check_margin_label = TRUE,
  .duplicates = c("error", "drop", "keep"),
  .sort = TRUE,
  .key = "data",
  .keep = FALSE
)







.data


A local data frame or a dtplyr step. Other lazy tables are not supported because nesting creates list columns.




.by


<tidy-select> Columns included in every grouping set. These columns never receive .margin_label.




.grouping


A grouping specification made with grouping_set(), grouping_sets(), rollup(), cube(), or grouping_spec(). NULL represents one empty grouping set.




.margin_label


A character scalar used to display columns omitted from a grouping set. The default is “Total”. Use NULL to keep typed missing values instead of inserting a display label.




.check_margin_label


A logical scalar. If TRUE, check whether the display label already occurs in a grouping column.




.duplicates


One of “error”, “drop”, or “keep”, controlling duplicate grouping sets after expansion.




.sort


A logical scalar. If TRUE (the default), sort by .by followed by grouping dimensions.




.key


A non-missing string naming the list column.




.keep


Should grouping columns also be kept inside each nested data frame?






For a local input, an ungrouped data frame with one list column. A dtplyr input returns a lazy dtplyr step until collected.



Other summarize and expand data with margins: nest_by_with_margins(), summarize_with_margins(), union_all_with_margins()




library("marginplyr")

nest_with_margins(
  mtcars,
  .grouping = rollup(cyl, vs)
)

# A tibble: 9 × 3
  cyl   vs    data             
  <chr> <chr> <list>           
1 4     0     <tibble [1 × 9]> 
2 4     1     <tibble [10 × 9]>
3 4     Total <tibble [11 × 9]>
4 6     0     <tibble [3 × 9]> 
5 6     1     <tibble [4 × 9]> 
6 6     Total <tibble [7 × 9]> 
7 8     0     <tibble [14 × 9]>
8 8     Total <tibble [14 × 9]>
9 Total Total <tibble [32 × 9]>

title: marginplyr
text:
grouping() and grouping_id() are contextual summary helpers for summarize_with_margins(). They distinguish values that are absent from a grouping set from ordinary missing values in the source data.



grouping(x)

grouping_id(...)







x


A bare grouping column.




…


Bare grouping columns.






grouping(x) returns 1L when x is absent from the grouping set and 0L otherwise. grouping_id(…) combines those flags as a bit mask; its last argument is the least-significant bit. It accepts between 1 and 31 distinct grouping columns.
Columns fixed by .by may also be passed to these helpers. Because a .by column belongs to every grouping set, grouping() is always 0L for it and it contributes a zero bit to grouping_id().



A numeric grouping flag or identifier when used inside summarize_with_margins(). Local results use integer vectors; database result types follow the backend.




library("marginplyr")

summarize_with_margins(
  mtcars,
  n = dplyr::n(),
  is_cyl_total = grouping(cyl),
  level = grouping_id(cyl, vs),
  .grouping = rollup(cyl, vs)
)

# A tibble: 9 × 5
  cyl   vs        n is_cyl_total level
  <chr> <chr> <int>        <int> <int>
1 4     0         1            0     0
2 4     1        10            0     0
3 4     Total    11            0     1
4 6     0         3            0     0
5 6     1         4            0     0
6 6     Total     7            0     1
7 8     0        14            0     0
8 8     Total    14            0     1
9 Total Total    32            1     3

title: marginplyr
text:
marginplyr brings SQL-style GROUPING SETS, ROLLUP, and CUBE to dplyr. The same grouping specification works with local data frames and lazy database tables, with "Total" as the default display label.






You can install the development version of marginplyr from GitHub with:
# install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("sayuks/marginplyr")



library(marginplyr)
#>
#> Attaching package: 'marginplyr'
#> The following object is masked from 'package:base':
#>
#>     grouping


summarize_with_margins(
  mtcars,
  n = dplyr::n(),
  mean_mpg = mean(mpg, na.rm = TRUE),
  level = grouping_id(cyl, vs),
  .grouping = rollup(cyl, vs)
)
#> # A tibble: 9 × 5
#>   cyl   vs        n mean_mpg level
#>   <chr> <chr> <int>    <dbl> <int>
#> 1 4     0         1     26       0
#> 2 4     1        10     26.7     0
#> 3 4     Total    11     26.7     1
#> 4 6     0         3     20.6     0
#> 5 6     1         4     19.1     0
#> 6 6     Total     7     19.7     1
#> 7 8     0        14     15.1     0
#> 8 8     Total    14     15.1     1
#> 9 Total Total    32     20.1     3



summarize_with_margins(
  mtcars,
  n = dplyr::n(),
  .grouping = grouping_sets(
    grouping_set(cyl, vs),
    grouping_set(cyl, gear),
    grouping_set()
  )
)
#> # A tibble: 14 × 4
#>    cyl   vs    gear      n
#>    <chr> <chr> <chr> <int>
#>  1 4     0     Total     1
#>  2 4     1     Total    10
#>  3 4     Total 3         1
#>  4 4     Total 4         8
#>  5 4     Total 5         2
#>  6 6     0     Total     3
#>  7 6     1     Total     4
#>  8 6     Total 3         2
#>  9 6     Total 4         4
#> 10 6     Total 5         1
#> 11 8     0     Total    14
#> 12 8     Total 3        12
#> 13 8     Total 5         2
#> 14 Total Total Total    32
grouping_spec() combines grouping families by Cartesian product. Composite dimensions stay together inside a rollup or cube:
summarize_with_margins(
  mtcars,
  n = dplyr::n(),
  .grouping = grouping_spec(
    rollup(cyl, vs),
    cube(grouping_set(gear, carb), am)
  )
) |>
  dplyr::slice_head(n = 12)
#> # A tibble: 12 × 6
#>    cyl   vs    gear  carb  am        n
#>    <chr> <chr> <chr> <chr> <chr> <int>
#>  1 4     0     5     2     1         1
#>  2 4     0     5     2     Total     1
#>  3 4     0     Total Total 1         1
#>  4 4     0     Total Total Total     1
#>  5 4     1     3     1     0         1
#>  6 4     1     3     1     Total     1
#>  7 4     1     4     1     1         4
#>  8 4     1     4     1     Total     4
#>  9 4     1     4     2     0         2
#> 10 4     1     4     2     1         2
#> 11 4     1     4     2     Total     4
#> 12 4     1     5     2     1         1



nest_by_with_margins() creates detail, subtotal, and grand-total groups that can be rendered directly as nested Quarto tabs with quartabs:
install.packages(c("knitr", "quartabs"))
# In a Quarto R chunk with: #| results: asis
mtcars |>
  nest_by_with_margins(.grouping = rollup(cyl, vs)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    report = list(
      data |>
        dplyr::summarize(
          rows = dplyr::n(),
          mean_mpg = mean(mpg)
        ) |>
        knitr::kable()
    )
  ) |>
  dplyr::ungroup() |>
  quartabs::render_tabset(
    tabset_vars = c(cyl, vs),
    output_vars = report
  )
The Get started guide contains a live, executable tabset.
DuckDB and PostgreSQL use one native GROUP BY GROUPING SETS query. Other lazy backends use a portable UNION ALL adapter with the same result semantics. grouping() and grouping_id() distinguish source missing values from rows produced by aggregation.
See Get started for more details.




Please note that the marginplyr project is released with a Contributor Code of Conduct. By contributing to this project, you agree to abide by its terms.

Examples of behavior that contributes to a positive environment for our community include:

Demonstrating empathy and kindness toward other people
Being respectful of differing opinions, viewpoints, and experiences
Giving and gracefully accepting constructive feedback

Focusing on what is best not just for us as individuals, but for the overall community

This Code of Conduct applies within all community spaces, and also applies when an individual is officially representing the community in public spaces. Examples of representing our community include using an official e-mail address, posting via an official social media account, or acting as an appointed representative at an online or offline event.



Instances of abusive, harassing, or otherwise unacceptable behavior may be reported to the community leaders responsible for enforcement at sayuks.dev@gmail.com. All complaints will be reviewed and investigated promptly and fairly.
All community leaders are obligated to respect the privacy and security of the reporter of any incident.



Community leaders will follow these Community Impact Guidelines in determining the consequences for any action they deem in violation of this Code of Conduct:


Community Impact: Use of inappropriate language or other behavior deemed unprofessional or unwelcome in the community.
Consequence: A private, written warning from community leaders, providing clarity around the nature of the violation and an explanation of why the behavior was inappropriate. A public apology may be requested.



Community Impact: A violation through a single incident or series of actions.
Consequence: A warning with consequences for continued behavior. No interaction with the people involved, including unsolicited interaction with those enforcing the Code of Conduct, for a specified period of time. This includes avoiding interactions in community spaces as well as external channels like social media. Violating these terms may lead to a temporary or permanent ban.

Community Impact: Demonstrating a pattern of violation of community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior, harassment of an individual, or aggression toward or disparagement of classes of individuals.
Consequence: A permanent ban from any sort of public interaction within the community.

Examples of representing our community include using an official e-mail address, posting via an official social media account, or acting as an appointed representative at an online or offline event.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text:
Instances of abusive, harassing, or otherwise unacceptable behavior may be reported to the community leaders responsible for enforcement at sayuks.dev@gmail.com. All complaints will be reviewed and investigated promptly and fairly.
All community leaders are obligated to respect the privacy and security of the reporter of any incident.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text:
Community leaders will follow these Community Impact Guidelines in determining the consequences for any action they deem in violation of this Code of Conduct:


Community Impact: Use of inappropriate language or other behavior deemed unprofessional or unwelcome in the community.
Consequence: A private, written warning from community leaders, providing clarity around the nature of the violation and an explanation of why the behavior was inappropriate. A public apology may be requested.



Community Impact: A violation through a single incident or series of actions.
Consequence: A warning with consequences for continued behavior. No interaction with the people involved, including unsolicited interaction with those enforcing the Code of Conduct, for a specified period of time. This includes avoiding interactions in community spaces as well as external channels like social media. Violating these terms may lead to a temporary or permanent ban.



Community Impact: A serious violation of community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior.
Consequence: A temporary ban from any sort of interaction or public communication with the community for a specified period of time. No public or private interaction with the people involved, including unsolicited interaction with those enforcing the Code of Conduct, is allowed during this period. Violating these terms may lead to a permanent ban.



Community Impact: Demonstrating a pattern of violation of community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior, harassment of an individual, or aggression toward or disparagement of classes of individuals.
Consequence: A permanent ban from any sort of public interaction within the community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text:
This Code of Conduct is adapted from the Contributor Covenant, version 2.1, available at https://www.contributor-covenant.org/version/2/1/code_of_conduct.html.
Community Impact Guidelines were inspired by [Mozilla’s code of conduct enforcement ladder][https://github.com/mozilla/inclusion].
For answers to common questions about this code of conduct, see the FAQ at https://www.contributor-covenant.org/faq. Translations are available at https://www.contributor-covenant.org/translations.

title: Get started
text: marginplyr brings SQL-style grouping sets to dplyr workflows. A single grouping specification can be executed locally, translated to native SQL, or expanded to a portable UNION ALL query.

title: Get started
text:
1 Installation
Install the development version from GitHub:

# install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("sayuks/marginplyr")

# Optional packages used by this guide and its Quarto tabset example
install.packages(c("knitr", "quartabs", "tibble"))


library(marginplyr)


Attaching package: 'marginplyr'


The following object is masked from 'package:base':

    grouping

library(dplyr)


Attaching package: 'dplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

library(tibble)


sales <- tribble(
  ~year, ~region, ~store, ~product, ~channel, ~amount,
  2024L, "East", "E1", "A", "Online", 10,
  2024L, "East", "E2", "B", "Store", 20,
  2024L, "West", "W1", "A", "Online", 15,
  2025L, "East", "E1", "A", "Store", 12,
  2025L, "West", "W1", NA_character_, "Online", 18
)

title: Get started
text:
2 The interface
.by contains fixed groups that occur in every result level. .grouping describes the dimensions that can be aggregated.

sales |>
  summarize_with_margins(
    total = sum(amount),
    .by = year,
    .grouping = rollup(region, store)
  )

# A tibble: 11 × 4
    year region store total
   <int> <chr>  <chr> <dbl>
 1  2024 East   E1       10
 2  2024 East   E2       20
 3  2024 East   Total    30
 4  2024 Total  Total    45
 5  2024 West   Total    15
 6  2024 West   W1       15
 7  2025 East   E1       12
 8  2025 East   Total    12
 9  2025 Total  Total    30
10  2025 West   Total    18
11  2025 West   W1       18


"Total" is a display label. Internally, grouping rows are identified from the grouping plan or SQL GROUPING() result, never by comparing this string.

title: Get started
text:
3 Arbitrary grouping sets
grouping_sets() forms the union of explicitly requested sets. grouping_set() with no columns is the empty grouping set and produces a grand total (or one total per fixed .by group).

sales |>
  summarize_with_margins(
    total = sum(amount),
    .grouping = grouping_sets(
      grouping_set(region, store),
      grouping_set(region, product),
      grouping_set()
    )
  )

# A tibble: 8 × 4
  region store product total
  <chr>  <chr> <chr>   <dbl>
1 East   E1    Total      22
2 East   E2    Total      20
3 East   Total A          22
4 East   Total B          20
5 Total  Total Total      75
6 West   Total A          15
7 West   Total <NA>       18
8 West   W1    Total      33

title: Get started
text:
4 ROLLUP
rollup(region, store) expands to the following sets:
(region, store)
(region)
()

sales |>
  summarize_with_margins(
    total = sum(amount),
    .grouping = rollup(region, store)
  )

# A tibble: 6 × 3
  region store total
  <chr>  <chr> <dbl>
1 East   E1       22
2 East   E2       20
3 East   Total    42
4 Total  Total    75
5 West   Total    33
6 West   W1       33

title: Get started
text:
5 CUBE
cube(product, channel) expands to every subset:
(product, channel)
(product)
(channel)
()

sales |>
  summarize_with_margins(
    total = sum(amount),
    .grouping = cube(product, channel)
  )

# A tibble: 10 × 3
   product channel total
   <chr>   <chr>   <dbl>
 1 A       Online     25
 2 A       Store      12
 3 A       Total      37
 4 B       Store      20
 5 B       Total      20
 6 Total   Online     43
 7 Total   Store      32
 8 Total   Total      75
 9 <NA>    Online     18
10 <NA>    Total      18

title: Get started
text:
6 Cartesian products
SQL combines comma-separated grouping items by Cartesian product. grouping_spec() provides the same operation in R.

sales |>
  summarize_with_margins(
    total = sum(amount),
    .by = year,
    .grouping = grouping_spec(
      rollup(region, store),
      cube(product, channel)
    )
  )

# A tibble: 53 × 6
    year region store product channel total
   <int> <chr>  <chr> <chr>   <chr>   <dbl>
 1  2024 East   E1    A       Online     10
 2  2024 East   E1    A       Total      10
 3  2024 East   E1    Total   Online     10
 4  2024 East   E1    Total   Total      10
 5  2024 East   E2    B       Store      20
 6  2024 East   E2    B       Total      20
 7  2024 East   E2    Total   Store      20
 8  2024 East   E2    Total   Total      20
 9  2024 East   Total A       Online     10
10  2024 East   Total A       Total      10
# ℹ 43 more rows


The compiler expands the product to concrete grouping sets before choosing a local or SQL execution adapter.

title: Get started
text:
7 Nested specifications
Grouping families can be nested in grouping_sets().

sales |>
  summarize_with_margins(
    total = sum(amount),
    .grouping = grouping_sets(
      rollup(region, store),
      cube(product, channel)
    ),
    .duplicates = "drop"
  )

# A tibble: 15 × 5
   region store product channel total
   <chr>  <chr> <chr>   <chr>   <dbl>
 1 East   E1    Total   Total      22
 2 East   E2    Total   Total      20
 3 East   Total Total   Total      42
 4 Total  Total A       Online     25
 5 Total  Total A       Store      12
 6 Total  Total A       Total      37
 7 Total  Total B       Store      20
 8 Total  Total B       Total      20
 9 Total  Total Total   Online     43
10 Total  Total Total   Store      32
11 Total  Total Total   Total      75
12 Total  Total <NA>    Online     18
13 Total  Total <NA>    Total      18
14 West   Total Total   Total      33
15 West   W1    Total   Total      33


Both families contain the empty set. The default .duplicates = "error" detects this; "drop" keeps the first occurrence and "keep" preserves every occurrence.

title: Get started
text:
8 Composite dimensions
A nested grouping_set() inside rollup() or cube() is one indivisible dimension.

locations <- tibble(
  country = c("JP", "JP", "US"),
  state = c("Tokyo", "Osaka", "CA"),
  year = c(2024L, 2025L, 2024L),
  amount = c(10, 20, 30)
)

locations |>
  summarize_with_margins(
    total = sum(amount),
    .grouping = cube(grouping_set(country, state), year)
  )

# A tibble: 9 × 4
  country state year  total
  <chr>   <chr> <chr> <dbl>
1 JP      Osaka 2025     20
2 JP      Osaka Total    20
3 JP      Tokyo 2024     10
4 JP      Tokyo Total    10
5 Total   Total 2024     40
6 Total   Total 2025     20
7 Total   Total Total    60
8 US      CA    2024     30
9 US      CA    Total    30


country and state are always included or aggregated together.

title: Get started
text:
9 Inspecting aggregation levels
grouping(x) returns 1 when x was aggregated away and 0 when it belongs to the active grouping set. grouping_id() combines those flags as a bit mask; the last argument is the least-significant bit.

sales |>
  summarize_with_margins(
    total = sum(amount),
    region_grouped = grouping(region),
    level = grouping_id(region, store, product),
    .grouping = grouping_spec(
      rollup(region, store),
      cube(product)
    )
  )

# A tibble: 17 × 6
   region store product total region_grouped level
   <chr>  <chr> <chr>   <dbl>          <int> <int>
 1 East   E1    A          22              0     0
 2 East   E1    Total      22              0     1
 3 East   E2    B          20              0     0
 4 East   E2    Total      20              0     1
 5 East   Total A          22              0     2
 6 East   Total B          20              0     2
 7 East   Total Total      42              0     3
 8 Total  Total A          37              1     6
 9 Total  Total B          20              1     6
10 Total  Total Total      75              1     7
11 Total  Total <NA>       18              1     6
12 West   Total A          15              0     2
13 West   Total Total      33              0     3
14 West   Total <NA>       18              0     2
15 West   W1    A          15              0     0
16 West   W1    Total      33              0     1
17 West   W1    <NA>       18              0     0


This also distinguishes source missing values from grouping rows. A source NA has a grouping bit of 0; an NA produced by aggregation has a bit of 1. Columns fixed with .by can also be passed to these helpers; because they are present in every grouping set, their grouping flag and bit are always 0.

title: Get started
text:
10 Labels and column types
Change the report label with .margin_label:

sales |>
  summarize_with_margins(
    total = sum(amount),
    .grouping = rollup(region, store),
    .margin_label = "All"
  )

# A tibble: 6 × 3
  region store total
  <chr>  <chr> <dbl>
1 All    All      75
2 East   All      42
3 East   E1       22
4 East   E2       20
5 West   All      33
6 West   W1       33


When a character label is used, grouping dimensions are returned as character columns; local factors are reconstructed with the label as their first level. Use .margin_label = NULL to keep original column types and typed missing values. In that mode, use grouping() or grouping_id() to tell source and aggregation missing values apart.
For local data, .check_margin_label = TRUE prevents an existing value such as "Total" from becoming visually ambiguous. Disable the check only when the grouping bits are also retained.

title: Get started
text:
11 Computed grouping columns
Grouping constructors accept column selections rather than arbitrary SQL expressions. Create an expression result first with mutate().

sales |>
  mutate(region_product = paste(region, product, sep = ":")) |>
  summarize_with_margins(
    total = sum(amount),
    .grouping = rollup(region_product)
  )

# A tibble: 5 × 2
  region_product total
  <chr>          <dbl>
1 East:A            22
2 East:B            20
3 Total             75
4 West:A            15
5 West:NA           18


This form also translates predictably for lazy database tables.

title: Get started
Get started
text:
13 Quarto tabset reports with quartabs
quartabs turns columns of a data frame into nested Quarto tabsets. It pairs naturally with nest_by_with_margins(): detail groups, subtotals, and the grand total become tabs backed by the corresponding rows of the original data.
The chunk option results: asis is required because render_tabset() emits Quarto tabset markup. The rendered tabs below are generated when this page is built; they are not a static screenshot.
tabbed_report <- sales |>
  nest_by_with_margins(
    .grouping = rollup(region, store)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    report = list(
      data |>
        summarize(
          rows = n(),
          amount = sum(amount)
        ) |>
        knitr::kable(col.names = c("Rows", "Amount"))
    )
  ) |>
  ungroup()

tabbed_report |>
  quartabs::render_tabset(
    tabset_vars = c(region, store),
    output_vars = report
  )

Tabs labelled "Total" are subtotal or grand-total levels created by the grouping plan. quartabs sorts tab labels, so their visual position follows the label ordering rather than the SQL grouping-set order.

title: Get started
text:
14 Database execution
DuckDB and PostgreSQL currently use native GROUP BY GROUPING SETS. Native queries use hidden GROUPING() flags and apply the display label in an outer query, preserving the input type seen by summary expressions. Other or unconfirmed SQL dialects use the UNION ALL adapter. This conservative capability policy favors equal results over assuming dialect support.

title: marginplyr 0.1.0
text:
marginplyr 0.1.0

Initial CRAN submission.
Added grouping_set(), grouping_sets(), rollup(), cube(), and grouping_spec() for arbitrary SQL-style grouping plans, including empty sets, nesting, Cartesian products, and composite dimensions.
Added contextual grouping() and grouping_id() summary helpers.
Added explicit duplicate-set policies: "error", "drop", and "keep".
Changed the default display label to "Total"; .margin_label = NULL preserves grouping-column types and typed missing values.
DuckDB and PostgreSQL use native GROUPING SETS; other backends use the portable UNION ALL adapter.
summarize_with_margins(), union_all_with_margins(), nest_with_margins(), and nest_by_with_margins() now share one normalized grouping-plan implementation.

title: marginplyr
text:
summarize_with_margins() extends dplyr::summarize() with grouping sets, rollups, cubes, totals, and subtotals. The same interface works with local data frames and lazy tables.



summarize_with_margins(
  .data,
  ...,
  .by = NULL,
  .grouping = NULL,
  .margin_label = "Total",
  .check_margin_label = is.data.frame(.data),
  .duplicates = c("error", "drop", "keep"),
  .sort = is.data.frame(.data)
)







.data


A data frame or lazy table.




…


Name-value pairs as used in dplyr::summarize(). Contextual helpers grouping() and grouping_id() can also be used here.




.by


<tidy-select> Columns included in every grouping set. These columns never receive .margin_label.




.grouping


A grouping specification made with grouping_set(), grouping_sets(), rollup(), cube(), or grouping_spec(). NULL represents one empty grouping set.




.margin_label


A character scalar used to display columns omitted from a grouping set. The default is “Total”. Use NULL to keep typed missing values instead of inserting a display label.




.check_margin_label


A logical scalar. If TRUE, check whether the display label already occurs in a grouping column.




.duplicates


One of “error”, “drop”, or “keep”, controlling duplicate grouping sets after expansion.




.sort


A logical scalar. If TRUE, sort by .by followed by grouping dimensions. It defaults to TRUE for local data frames and FALSE for lazy tables.






grouping_sets() forms a union of grouping families. grouping_spec() combines its arguments by Cartesian product, matching comma-separated SQL GROUP BY items. grouping_set() is also used to keep multiple columns together as one composite dimension inside rollup() or cube().
Grouping specifications accept column selections, not arbitrary SQL expressions. Create computed grouping columns with dplyr::mutate() first.
Confirmed SQL backends use one GROUP BY GROUPING SETS query. Other lazy backends use a portable UNION ALL adapter with the same semantics.



An ungrouped data frame, or a lazy table when .data is lazy.



Other summarize and expand data with margins: nest_by_with_margins(), nest_with_margins(), union_all_with_margins()




library("marginplyr")

summarize_with_margins(
  mtcars,
  n = dplyr::n(),
  mean_mpg = mean(mpg),
  .grouping = rollup(cyl, vs)
)

# A tibble: 9 × 4
  cyl   vs        n mean_mpg
  <chr> <chr> <int>    <dbl>
1 4     0         1     26  
2 4     1        10     26.7
3 4     Total    11     26.7
4 6     0         3     20.6
5 6     1         4     19.1
6 6     Total     7     19.7
7 8     0        14     15.1
8 8     Total    14     15.1
9 Total Total    32     20.1

summarize_with_margins(
  mtcars,
  n = dplyr::n(),
  level = grouping_id(cyl, vs, gear),
  .grouping = grouping_spec(rollup(cyl, vs), cube(gear))
)

# A tibble: 30 × 5
   cyl   vs    gear      n level
   <chr> <chr> <chr> <int> <int>
 1 4     0     5         1     0
 2 4     0     Total     1     1
 3 4     1     3         1     0
 4 4     1     4         8     0
 5 4     1     5         1     0
 6 4     1     Total    10     1
 7 4     Total 3         1     2
 8 4     Total 4         8     2
 9 4     Total 5         2     2
10 4     Total Total    11     3
# ℹ 20 more rows

summarize_with_margins(
  mtcars,
  n = dplyr::n(),
  .grouping = grouping_sets(
    grouping_set(cyl, vs),
    grouping_set(cyl, gear),
    grouping_set()
  )
)

# A tibble: 14 × 4
   cyl   vs    gear      n
   <chr> <chr> <chr> <int>
 1 4     0     Total     1
 2 4     1     Total    10
 3 4     Total 3         1
 4 4     Total 4         8
 5 4     Total 5         2
 6 6     0     Total     3
 7 6     1     Total     4
 8 6     Total 3         2
 9 6     Total 4         4
10 6     Total 5         1
11 8     0     Total    14
12 8     Total 3        12
13 8     Total 5         2
14 Total Total Total    32

title: marginplyr
text:
These constructors describe SQL-style grouping operations for the .grouping argument of summarize_with_margins() and related verbs.



grouping_set(...)

grouping_sets(...)

rollup(...)

cube(...)

grouping_spec(...)







…


Bare columns, tidy-select expressions, or nested grouping specifications as appropriate for the constructor.








grouping_set() creates one grouping set. With no columns it represents the empty set ().


grouping_sets() forms the union of its arguments.


rollup() creates hierarchical prefixes.


cube() creates every subset of its dimensions.


grouping_spec() forms the Cartesian product of its arguments, like comma-separated SQL GROUP BY items.


A grouping_set() nested directly inside rollup() or cube() is a composite dimension. Its columns are added or removed together.



A grouping specification for use in .grouping.




library("marginplyr")

rollup(region, store)

<marginplyr grouping specification: rollup>

cube(product, channel)

<marginplyr grouping specification: cube>

grouping_sets(
  grouping_set(region, store),
  grouping_set(region, product),
  grouping_set()
)

<marginplyr grouping specification: sets>

grouping_spec(
  rollup(region, store),
  cube(product, channel)
)

<marginplyr grouping specification: product>

cube(grouping_set(country, state), year)

<marginplyr grouping specification: cube>

title: marginplyr
text:
union_all_with_margins() emits one copy of each input row for every grouping set, replacing omitted grouping dimensions with .margin_label. This is analogous to vertically combining the grouping branches with SQL UNION ALL.



union_all_with_margins(
  .data,
  .by = NULL,
  .grouping = NULL,
  .margin_label = "Total",
  .check_margin_label = is.data.frame(.data),
  .duplicates = c("error", "drop", "keep"),
  .sort = is.data.frame(.data)
)

title: marginplyr
text:
union_all_with_margins() emits one copy of each input row for every grouping set, replacing omitted grouping dimensions with .margin_label. This is analogous to vertically combining the grouping branches with SQL UNION ALL.



union_all_with_margins(
  .data,
  .by = NULL,
  .grouping = NULL,
  .margin_label = "Total",
  .check_margin_label = is.data.frame(.data),
  .duplicates = c("error", "drop", "keep"),
  .sort = is.data.frame(.data)
)







.data


A data frame or lazy table.




.by


<tidy-select> Columns included in every grouping set. These columns never receive .margin_label.




.grouping


A grouping specification made with grouping_set(), grouping_sets(), rollup(), cube(), or grouping_spec(). NULL represents one empty grouping set.




.margin_label


A character scalar used to display columns omitted from a grouping set. The default is “Total”. Use NULL to keep typed missing values instead of inserting a display label.




.check_margin_label


A logical scalar. If TRUE, check whether the display label already occurs in a grouping column.




.duplicates


One of “error”, “drop”, or “keep”, controlling duplicate grouping sets after expansion.




.sort


A logical scalar. If TRUE, sort by .by followed by grouping dimensions. It defaults to TRUE for local data frames and FALSE for lazy tables.






An ungrouped data frame, or a lazy table when .data is lazy.



Other summarize and expand data with margins: nest_by_with_margins(), nest_with_margins(), summarize_with_margins()
